text: The surgeon introduces a varus needle and performs irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm proper placement. Gas insufflation is completed at fifteen millimeters of mercury pressure. The incision is enlarged to eleven millimeters, and an optical trocar is introduced through the inferior crease of the umbilicus. The telescope is inserted, revealing the left side, which appears normal with no hernia. On the right side, the undescended testis and fibrotic tissue are visible. The surgeon uses a ligature to cut the gubernaculum during the orchidectomy.

The surgeon separates the testis and epididymis, identifying the vas deferens medially and spermatic vessels laterally. The orchidectomy is performed using a ligature, and the testis with epididymis is placed in an endo bag for removal through the umbilical port. The surgeon prepares to start transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair, positioning six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring at the two o'clock position.

The surgeon prepares the preperitoneal pocket by carefully separating the peritoneum above the arc of the sac, reaching up to the medial umbilical ligament. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible, requiring caution during dissection. Using a harmonic device, the surgeon creates space for the mesh by performing lateral, medial, and sac dissections, separating the transversalis fascia from the peritoneum to ensure a proper pocket for the 15x15 cm mesh.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection using three ports: two contralateral 5mm ports and one 10mm umbilical port. The bladder is pushed down into the retropubic space below the pubic symphysis, revealing the shiny, pearly white Cooper ligament. Following lateral and medial dissection, the surgeon proceeds with sac dissection, separating the transverse fascia from the sac. The external iliac artery and vein are clearly visible, and the pocket is enlarged to accommodate a large mesh.

The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, with the trapezoid of disaster visible above the space of Bogros. The preperitoneal pocket is enlarged to accommodate the mesh. The mesh is introduced and spread medially and laterally, with its inferior medial edge positioned on the Cooper's ligament. The mesh is fixed to the Cooper's ligament and laterally, followed by peritonealization. The pressure is reduced to facilitate the procedure, ensuring the mesh is covered by the peritoneum.
